title: Shaders in Figma: create the effects and fills you've always wanted
speakers: Yassir Solomah (Software Engineering Manager, Figma), Piers Cowburn (Director of Engineering, Expressive Design, Figma), Rogie King (Product Designer, Figma)
key_points: Figma is launching native shaders, implemented as 'secure WebGPU intra-layer render pipelines' running custom code directly on the GPU, exposed as two types: shader effects (stacked in the effects panel) and shader fills (usable anywhere a fill works, text, strokes, brush strokes).

Shaders are generated and edited via an AI agent: you describe an effect in plain language and Figma produces it, including on-canvas handles (point, line, angle, radius), sliders, gradients, selects, switches, and color points. Shipped template effects include pixel stretch, gooey merge, chromatic metal, lens distort, dither, pixelate, and Riso.

The power comes from stacking and recombining effects on a base input, Piers demoed a corner-peel plus a shapes-mosaic that maps brightness values to shapes/colors, plus a Figma-logo example with lens distortion, time-offset duplicates and blend modes (multiply/overlay/difference) to 'play jazz' into unexpected outcomes.

Technical constraints: shaders are raster effects rendered at frame/layer size (no infinite zoom, they pixelate when zoomed), capped at a maximum 2048 texture size, and time-varying shaders sync their play head to the motion clip in motion mode.

Code is fully exportable via MCP/dev mode, you get a custom effect wrapper plus raw source (a one-time setup function, a per-frame render function, and a defined properties array describing the UI) with an included runtime, because 'your code is yours to take.' Inspiration site: shaders.figma.com.

quotes.text: If design was the body, expression was the soul.
quotes.talk: Shaders in Figma: create the effects and fills you've always wanted
